patient.name: Victor Lazzaro
patient.chiefComplaint: Severe chest pain and shortness of breath after high-speed MVC, struck chest on steering wheel

history.hpi: 55-year-old male, unrestrained driver in a high-speed (estimated 55 mph) front-end collision with a fixed object. Driver's airbag did NOT deploy (older vehicle). Hit the steering wheel with his chest. Found at the scene with difficulty breathing and obvious chest wall deformity. Complains of severe left-sided chest pain with every breath.
history.events: High-speed MVC, front-end collision, unrestrained driver, chest impact with steering wheel. Extricated by fire from the driver's seat.

scene.dispatch: Medic 3, respond code 3 to eastbound I-43 milepost 72 for a single-vehicle MVC, driver trapped, chest injury.
scene.location: Highway shoulder. Driver extricated onto a backboard next to a crumpled passenger vehicle.
scene.safety: Lanes closed by law enforcement. Fire on scene. Fuel spill controlled.
scene.bystanders: Fire crew with extrication complete; witness who saw the impact is being interviewed by police
visualCues: Middle-aged male supine on a backboard, obviously in pain
Visible chest wall deformity on the left with paradoxical movement during each breath
Speaking in short phrases, splinting with every breath
Abrasions across the anterior chest from the steering wheel
Breath sounds diminished on the left
Extrication complete — patient on long board with c-collar

phases.name: Arrival — Flail Chest with Respiratory Compromise
phases.description: Flail chest is visually dramatic but the underlying pulmonary contusion is the real killer — it's an evolving inflammatory injury that will worsen over the next 24 hours. Positive pressure ventilation and aggressive pain management to allow deeper breathing are the priorities. Avoid heavy fluid resuscitation which will worsen contusion edema.

clinicalPresentation.breathing: Rapid shallow at 32/min with obvious paradoxical chest wall movement on the left anterior chest (segment moves inward with inspiration); decreased breath sounds on the left; crepitus palpable over the flail segment
clinicalPresentation.motorFunction: Splinting with every breath, very uncomfortable
otherFindings: Paradoxical flail segment on left anterior chest
Subcutaneous crepitus over the flail segment
Underlying pulmonary contusion — the real threat
Patient is splinting, which worsens atelectasis

expectedActions.action: Apply MARCH primary survey; no massive hemorrhage noted
Maintain cervical collar and long board immobilization (mechanism)
Apply high-flow oxygen via non-rebreather; target SpO2 ≥94%
Auscultate breath sounds bilaterally and monitor for tension pneumothorax — be ready with needle decompression equipment on the left
Administer permissive hypotensive fluid strategy (target SBP 90 mmHg, palpable radial): only bolus if SBP <90 or HR >120 with signs of hypoperfusion; start with 500 mL
expectedActions.rationale: Aggressive fluid worsens pulmonary contusion edema. Permissive hypotension is appropriate in isolated chest trauma without head injury.

expectedActions.action: Administer Fentanyl 1-2 mcg/kg IV (92-184 mcg for 92 kg patient) for pain; titrate to effect; may repeat
expectedActions.rationale: Aggressive analgesia is the most important intervention — allows deeper breathing and reduces splinting/atelectasis.

expectedActions.action: Consider CPAP starting at 5 cmH2O — provides an internal 'splint' for the flail segment and treats underlying contusion atelectasis
Do NOT tape or pad the flail segment externally — causes restriction and worsens ventilation
Apply cardiac monitor, SpO2, waveform capnography

phases.name: Default — Improvement with Analgesia and CPAP
phases.description: With adequate fentanyl and CPAP, the patient is able to take deeper breaths, SpO2 improves, and splinting decreases. Rapid transport to trauma center continues. Vigilance for tension pneumothorax remains.
phases.triggerCondition: Stabilization after pain control and CPAP

clinicalPresentation.breathing: CPAP in place, slower respiratory rate, deeper tidal volumes, less splinting
otherFindings: Flail segment still paradoxical but less dramatic with CPAP

expectedActions.action: Maintain CPAP without breaking the seal
Continue titration of fentanyl for ongoing pain
Expedited transport to Level I trauma center; scene time <10 minutes
Reassess breath sounds frequently for developing tension pneumothorax or hemothorax

phases.name: Branch: Tension Pneumothorax Development
phases.description: During transport the patient deteriorates with sudden hypotension, absent left breath sounds, and tracheal deviation. Classic tension pneumothorax from the rib fractures and underlying lung injury. Needle decompression is indicated.

clinicalPresentation.breathing: Absent breath sounds on the left, tracheal deviation to the right, jugular venous distention
otherFindings: Classic tension pneumothorax presentation — act immediately, don't wait for confirmation

expectedActions.action: Perform needle decompression on the left — 4th or 5th intercostal space at the anterior axillary line (preferred) with a large-bore needle/catheter
expectedActions.rationale: Anterior axillary line at 4th/5th ICS has better success than 2nd ICS midclavicular due to chest wall thickness.

expectedActions.action: Reassess breath sounds and hemodynamics after decompression; be prepared for a second decompression if symptoms recur
Expedited transport continues with crew prepared for further interventions

criticalActions: Permissive hypotensive resuscitation — avoid over-fluid loading
Aggressive pain management with Fentanyl 1-2 mcg/kg IV, titrated
Apply CPAP as an internal splint for the flail segment
Do NOT tape or pad the flail segment externally
Reassess breath sounds continuously for tension pneumothorax
Expedited transport to Level I trauma center with scene time <10 min
expectedActions: Have needle decompression equipment immediately accessible
bonusActions: Articulate that pulmonary contusion is the real threat, not the flail segment
Choose CPAP over intubation when mental status permits
Know the preferred needle decompression location (4th-5th ICS AAL)

learningObjectives: Apply MATC General Trauma Management MARCH approach
Manage flail chest with CPAP and aggressive analgesia
Understand that pulmonary contusion is the underlying injury
discussionQuestions: Why is CPAP effective in flail chest?
Why do we NOT tape or pad the flail segment externally?
How does pulmonary contusion evolve over time and why is transport timing critical?
What are the hemodynamic signs of tension pneumothorax and where do you decompress?
How does pain management improve respiratory mechanics in chest trauma?
commonPitfalls: Underdosing analgesia because 'respiratory depression' is feared
Over-resuscitating with crystalloid and worsening contusion edema
Delaying needle decompression until classic signs are 'definite'
Taping the flail segment and worsening ventilation
Intubating instead of trying CPAP first
keyTakeaways: The flail is visible; the contusion is what kills
Pain control is ventilator support in chest trauma
CPAP stabilizes the flail from the inside
Permissive hypotension protects the contusion from worsening
